evidence.title: Clinical Guidance for Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis | Group A Strep | CDC
evidence.snippet: Cephalexin, oral 20 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 500 mg/dose) for 10 days Cefadroxil, oral 30 mg/kg once daily (maximum = 1 g) for 10 days Clindamycin, oral 7 mg/kg/dose 3 times daily (maximum = 300 mg/dose) for 10 days Azithromycin, oral 12 mg/kg once (maximum = 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg (max=250 mg) once daily for the next 4 days Clarithromycin, oral 7.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 250 mg/dose) for 10 days Antibiotic resistance There's never been a report of a clinical isolate of group A strep bacteria that's resistant to penicillin or cephalosporins.

evidence.title: Strep throat: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Certain other antibiotics may also work against the strep bacteria. Antibiotics should be taken for 10 days, even though symptoms are often gone within a few days. The following tips may help your sore throat feel better:

evidence.title: This Patient Group Direction (PGD) must only be used by registered healthcare
Strep A
evidence.snippet: If you or your child has a strep A infection, or are prescribed antibiotics for strep A, you should stay away from nursery, school or work for 24 hours after you start taking antibiotics. This will help stop the infection spreading to other people.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 2-day history of sore throat accompanied by fever and painful swallowing. She reports swollen glands in her neck and visible white patches on her tonsils. There is a known exposure to strep throat from her son last week. She denies cough, nasal congestion, rash, joint pain, or difficulty breathing or swallowing saliva.

O: Phone-only encounter; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute streptococcal pharyngitis given the classic presentation of sore throat with fever, tonsillar exudates, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, and recent exposure to a confirmed case.
Differential:
1. Streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Peritonsillar abscess (less likely without severe dysphagia or respiratory distress)

P: - First-line medication: Penicillin V 500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: No allergies reported; no pregnancy, renal, or liver disease; monitor for allergic reactions
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing, severe swallowing difficulty, or worsening symptoms occur; seek emergency care if airway compromise develops
- Testing: Throat culture or rapid antigen detection test for group A streptococcus if available to confirm diagnosis
